does: This paper identifies and characterizes two largely overlooked AI safety risks: deskilling (cognitive atrophy from over-relying on GenAI systems) and addiction (psychological dependence on GenAI). The authors document that while mainstream AI safety research focuses on discrimination, harmful content, information hazards, and malicious misuse, public discourse increasingly emphasizes threats to human cognition and mental wellbeing. They quantify this discrepancy by analyzing the gap between academic safety literature and public concern, then propose initial frameworks for how AI safety and alignment research could begin addressing these cognitive and mental health impacts at scale, establishing deskilling and addiction as legitimate safety concerns warranting systematic investigation.
connects: This work bridges a gap between the traditional AI safety agenda—shaped by high-profile concerns about discrimination, content moderation, and dual-use risks—and emerging public concerns about human cognitive autonomy. It builds on earlier work examining technology's effects on human skill development and critical thinking, drawing implicit connections to research on technological dependency and digital well-being from psychology and human-computer interaction. The paper acknowledges that while AI alignment literature has matured substantially around narrow harms, it has largely neglected the diffuse, population-level effects of cognitive offloading, paralleling how digital technology research initially overlooked addiction and attention economy concerns before they became central in tech ethics discourse.
enables: This framing opens new research directions for AI safety by expanding the threat model beyond acute harms to include chronic, systemic degradation of human cognitive capabilities and psychological resilience. It enables development of new evaluation metrics, design principles, and policy interventions that measure and mitigate skill atrophy and behavioral dependence. The work could motivate longitudinal studies tracking how GenAI adoption correlates with changes in critical thinking, problem-solving ability, and mental health outcomes. It also sets up opportunities for designing GenAI systems with built-in friction, transparency about offloading consequences, and features that preserve or enhance human agency—positioning cognitive autonomy preservation as a core safety objective alongside traditional harms reduction.
